articles.title: Native Nations Fought in the American Revolution to Protect Their Homelands — But the New Nation Took Their Land Anyway
articles.summary: When the American Revolution broke out, the Haudenosaunee Confederacy — six Native nations in the Mohawk Valley — tried to stay neutral. But both the British and the colonists pressured them to choose sides. The Oneida and Tuscarora allied with the colonists, while the Mohawk, Onondaga, Cayuga, and Seneca fought for the British. At the Battle of Oriskany on August 6, 1777, Native soldiers fought each other for the first time in centuries. Historians say Native nations joined the war strategically, hoping to protect their lands from settler encroachment. After the war, the United States seized their territory anyway, exposing the contradiction of a nation born from a fight for freedom built on dispossession.

articles.title: Defending Champion Carlos Alcaraz Withdraws from French Open Due to Wrist Injury
articles.summary: Two-time defending champion Carlos Alcaraz will miss the French Open next month due to a right wrist injury sustained in Barcelona. The 22-year-old world number two underwent tests and decided to also skip the Italian Open to recover. Alcaraz, who won the Australian Open this year to become the youngest man to complete a career Grand Slam, said the decision was cautious. His rival Jannik Sinner, who recently took the world number one ranking, expressed sympathy and hopes Alcaraz returns for Wimbledon. Sinner now has a clear path to win the French Open and achieve his own career Grand Slam. Alcaraz has only missed one Grand Slam since 2021.

articles.title: FDA Approves First Gene Therapy for Inherited Deafness — A 'Landmark Moment'
articles.summary: The FDA has approved Otarmeni, the first gene therapy for inherited deafness, targeting mutations in the OTOF gene. The one-time treatment uses harmless viruses to deliver working copies of the gene into the inner ear. In a trial of 20 participants, 16 showed improved hearing within six months, with some able to hear whispers. The therapy is approved for children and adults with OTOF-related hearing loss who haven't had cochlear implants in the treated ear. The manufacturer, Regeneron, says the treatment itself will be free to U.S. patients. Scientists call it a breakthrough that could accelerate development of future genetic therapies for hearing loss.
